name: Caitanya Mahaprabhu
dates_notes: Multiple near-contemporary biographies (Caitanya-Caritāmṛta, Caitanya-Bhāgavata); Mughal-period attestation.

dates_evidence.description: Mughal-period manuscript colophons attest his biographical span.

lineage_polemical.note: Reported debate-tradition with the Nimbārka-line scholar Keśava-Kāśmīrī.

core_thesis: Caitanya's position — codified by his disciples as *acintya-bhedābheda* — holds that Brahman, identified with Kṛṣṇa, stands to the *jīva* and the world in a relation of simultaneous *bheda* and *abheda* that is *acintya* (inconceivable to dialectical reason) by virtue of *acintya-śakti*, the inconceivable power proper to Bhagavān. This is not an abandonment of logic but a positive metaphysical claim: dialectic, which works by *bheda*/*abheda* exclusion, cannot adjudicate at the level of the relation between absolute Bhagavān and his eternal *śaktis*; the relation is real, knowable through *bhakti* and *śabda*, but not derivable by *tarka* alone. *Bhagavān* alone is *svayaṃ-bhagavān* and his *śaktis* — *cit*, *jīva-māyā*, and *acit-māyā* — are simultaneously identical with him and irreducibly distinct. The operative soteriology is *bhakti-rasa* with *nāma-saṅkīrtana* as its principal mode; liberation is eternal devotional participation in Kṛṣṇa-līlā, structured by *rasa* (*śānta*, *dāsya*, *sakhya*, *vātsalya*, *mādhurya*), and never the dissolution of devotee into deity.

engaged_works.title: Shikshashtakam
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored eight-verse hymn — the only text indisputably authored by Caitanya himself.
engaged_works.summary: The *Śikṣāṣṭakam* is the only text indisputably composed by Caitanya himself — eight Sanskrit verses preserved within the *Caitanya-Caritāmṛta* of Kṛṣṇadāsa Kavirāja. Compressed and personal, it is doctrinally consequential out of all proportion to its length: it states the supremacy of *nāma-saṅkīrtana* (the chanted divine name) as the means of cleansing consciousness in the present *yuga*; the devotional virtues of humility (*tṛṇād api sunīcena*), forbearance (*taror iva sahiṣṇunā*), absence of self-honour, and honour to others; the *jīva*'s status as eternal servant of Kṛṣṇa (*ayi nanda-tanuja kiṅkaraṃ*); the experiential intensity of *viraha* (separation) as itself a high mode of *bhakti*; and the refusal of all selfish ends — wealth, followers, even *mokṣa* construed as personal release — in favour of *ahaitukī bhakti*. The Gosvāmīs treat the verses as the doctrinal seed of the entire Gauḍīya theology of *rasa* and *prema* they go on to elaborate.

engaged_works.title: Doctrine recorded by disciples and biographers
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Caitanya's doctrine survives mainly as recorded by Rūpa, Sanātana, Jīva, and the Caitanya-Caritāmṛta biographer Kṛṣṇadāsa Kavirāja.
engaged_works.summary: Caitanya wrote no bhāṣya. His doctrine is preserved in three converging streams of disciple-record: (i) the biographical literature, principally Kṛṣṇadāsa Kavirāja's *Caitanya-Caritāmṛta* and Vṛndāvana-dāsa's *Caitanya-Bhāgavata*, both in Bengali with embedded Sanskrit verse, which preserve extended doctrinal dialogues — most famously the conversation with Rāmānanda Rāya on the gradation of *sādhana* and *rasa*, and the *daśa-mūla* teachings; (ii) the systematic theology of the Six Gosvāmīs of Vṛndāvana, who under Caitanya's commission articulate his position as *acintya-bhedābheda* with *acintya-śakti* as its load-bearing concept; and (iii) the liturgical and ritual code (Sanātana's *Hari-Bhakti-Vilāsa*) that fixes the school's practice. Reading Caitanya's doctrine therefore requires triangulation across hagiographical, theological, and ritual sources, with the *Śikṣāṣṭakam* as the only fixed first-person datum and Jīva's *Ṣaṭ-Sandarbha* as the canonical systematic articulation.
